title: Meeting Filipinos for the first time
subtitle: Warm greetings open every door here
context: Filipinos are famously friendly. A simple greeting in Tagalog — even just 'Kumusta' — earns you smiles and patient help. Use these when you meet someone new at a sari-sari store, on a jeepney, or visiting a friend's home.
sample_reply:
You: Kumusta po! Magandang umaga.
Local: Magandang umaga rin po! Kumusta ka?
You: Mabuti naman, salamat.

title: Ordering at a karinderya
subtitle: Local eateries rarely hand out menus
context: Karinderyas (small local eateries) usually have food laid out behind the counter — no menus, no English. You point and ask. A few polite Tagalog phrases make the whole exchange feel friendlier, and locals will happily teach you new words mid-order.
sample_reply:
You: Magandang umaga po! Pakiabot po ng menu.
Server: Eto po, ano gusto niyo?
You: Adobo po, walang sibuyas. Salamat!

start.speaker: Vendor
start.line: Magandang umaga po! Ano po ang order ninyo?
start.translation: Good morning! What would you like to order?

replies.text: Magandang umaga po. Pwede po bang makita ang menu?
replies.translation: Good morning. Could I see the menu?

menu_handed.speaker: Vendor
menu_handed.line: Walang menu po — dito lang tingnan. Mayroon kaming adobo, sinigang, lumpia, at kanin.
menu_handed.translation: No menu — just look here. We have adobo, sinigang, lumpia, and rice.

ask_specialty.speaker: Vendor
ask_specialty.line: Ang adobo po. Sikat dito sa amin.

replies.text: Salamat. Pwede po bang magtingin muna?
replies.translation: Thanks. May I look around first?

asked_price.speaker: Vendor
asked_price.line: Tatlumpung piso po. May sahog na baboy.

finished.speaker: Vendor
finished.line: Salamat po! Sandali lang, ihahanda ko na.
finished.translation: Thank you! One moment, I'll prepare it.
finished.end_message: You ordered like a regular. Sarap!

title: Getting lost in the city
subtitle: Find your way when Google Maps fails
context: Metro Manila streets aren't always well-labeled, and signs mix English, Tagalog, and old Spanish names. When you're lost, locals are your best map. Approach politely and you'll often get walked to the right spot.
sample_reply:
You: Pakiusap, pwede mo ba akong tulungan? Nasaan ang MRT station?
Local: Diretso lang po, may signage doon.
You: Salamat po!

replies.text: Opo, pakiusap, pwede mo ba akong tulungan?
replies.translation: Yes — please, can you help me?

replies.text: Naliligaw ako. Nasaan ang MRT station?
replies.translation: I'm lost. Where is the MRT station?

directions_mrt.speaker: Local
directions_mrt.line: Diretso po lang, mga limang minutong lakad. May signage doon.
directions_mrt.translation: Just straight, about five minutes' walk. There's signage.

replies.text: Pwede po ba ninyong samahan ako?

directions_market.speaker: Local
directions_market.line: Sa kanan po, malapit lang. Sa kabilang block.
directions_market.translation: To the right, very close. Next block.

restaurant.speaker: Local
restaurant.line: May Jollibee sa kanto, o karinderya doon sa kanan.
restaurant.translation: There's a Jollibee at the corner, or a karinderya to the right.

walked_together.speaker: Local
walked_together.line: Sige po, tara. Malapit lang naman.
walked_together.translation: Sure, let's go. It's near anyway.

declined.speaker: Local
declined.end_message: Sometimes it's nice to figure it out alone.

finished.speaker: Local
finished.end_message: You got the directions you needed. Walang problema.

finished_walked.speaker: Local
finished_walked.line: Eto na po, ito ang station.
finished_walked.translation: Here we are — this is the station.
finished_walked.end_message: Filipinos will often walk you the whole way. Bayanihan!
